title: Spaghetti al limone
subtitle: Lemon, butter, and Parmesan — a ten-minute pasta
excerpt: Lemon zest, a knob of butter, and aged Parmesan over spaghetti. No cream, no technique beyond patience with the pasta water. The Amalfi Coast version of a quick weeknight dinner, and one of the fastest pastas worth making.
sourceNotes: Pasta al limone is a Campanian and Sicilian preparation, most associated with the Amalfi Coast where citrus has been grown commercially since the medieval period. The simplest versions appear in Artusi's correspondence notes on southern Italian cookery (La scienza in cucina, 1891, public domain). Modern iterations typically add cream, but the original is butter, cheese, and zest only.

content.text: This is as simple as pasta gets. Three or four ingredients, fifteen minutes, and the result is clean and sharp in a way that cream-based lemon pasta never is. The acid cuts through the fat of the butter; the Parmesan provides depth; the pasta water pulls everything together. Use good, unwaxed lemons — the zest is doing most of the work.

The method works by building an emulsion in the pan rather than making a sauce separately. Butter, pasta water, and cheese combine over gentle heat into something glossy. Do not rush it with high heat or the butter will split and the pasta will clump.

items.prepNote: zest of both, juice of one

content.text: Bring a large pan of generously salted water to a boil. Add the {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti and cook until 2 minutes short of the al dente time on the packet. Scoop out 200 ml of pasta water before draining.

While the pasta finishes cooking, put the lemon zest in a wide frying pan over low heat with 100 ml of the pasta water. Let it come to a gentle simmer. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}} cubes and swirl the pan until the butter melts and the liquid becomes slightly creamy — about 2 minutes.

Add the drained pasta to the pan. Toss over low heat for 1 minute. Take the pan off the heat and add the {{parmesan}} of Parmesan and the lemon juice from one lemon. Toss again, adding a splash more pasta water if the sauce looks tight. The pasta should be glossy, not dry. Scatter over the parsley if using. Check seasoning and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Sauce is greasy rather than glossy
items.cause: Heat too high when adding butter, causing the emulsion to break
items.fix: Keep the pan over the lowest flame; the butter should melt slowly into the pasta water

items.symptom: Pasta is dry and the cheese clumps
items.cause: Parmesan added over heat, or not enough pasta water
items.fix: Take the pan off the heat entirely before adding the cheese; work in pasta water a tablespoon at a time

items.cause: Too much zest, or waxed lemons used — the wax traps bitter compounds
items.fix: Use unwaxed lemons; scrub waxed ones vigorously under hot water and dry before zesting

items.cause: Too much lemon juice, or the butter quantity was reduced
items.fix: Add juice in stages; the butter rounds the sharpness — don't reduce it

content.text: Spaghetti al limone belongs to the Campania coast, where Sfusato Amalfitano lemons (enormous, fragrant, low-acid) have been grown on terraced hillsides since Arab traders introduced cultivation to the region in the ninth century. The dish turns up in Positano trattorias as a summer first course, sometimes scattered with fresh basil rather than parsley, occasionally enriched with a spoon of cream in tourist-facing restaurants. The cream is a concession; the original is leaner and sharper.
